---
title: "Jointly fitting multiple data sources"
author: "Nowcasting and forecasting of infectious disease dynamics"
engine: knitr
format:
  revealjs:
    output: slides/multiple-data-sources.html
    footer: "Jointly fitting multiple data sources"
    chalkboard: true
    slide-level: 3
---

```{r setup, include = FALSE}
library("nfidd.nowcasting")
library("dplyr")
library("tidyr")
library("ggplot2")
set.seed(123)
theme_set(theme_minimal(base_size = 16))
```

### Building models iteratively

In this course we have been building models **iteratively** — each session added a piece and checked it.

### This is the Bayesian workflow

A *Bayesian workflow* is the iterative loop of **building**, **checking**, and **revising** a model.

![Bayesian Workflow (Gelman et al., 2026)](figures/book-image.png){height="340px"}

### The workflow loop

```{=html}
<div style="display:flex;justify-content:center;margin-top:0.4em;">
<svg viewBox="0 0 640 300" width="100%" style="max-width:520px;font-family:inherit;" role="img" aria-label="The Bayesian workflow as an iterative loop: build a model, check it, revise it, and repeat.">
  <defs>
    <marker id="wf-arrow" viewBox="0 0 10 10" refX="8.5" refY="5" markerWidth="7" markerHeight="7" orient="auto-start-reverse">
      <path d="M0,0 L10,5 L0,10 z" fill="#5b6b7b"/>
    </marker>
  </defs>
  <g stroke="#5b6b7b" stroke-width="2" fill="none" marker-end="url(#wf-arrow)">
    <path d="M395,90 Q478,124 470,190"/>
    <path d="M405,234 Q320,270 237,234"/>
    <path d="M170,190 Q162,124 245,90"/>
  </g>
  <g text-anchor="middle">
    <rect x="245" y="36" width="150" height="50" rx="9" fill="#2c7fb8" stroke="#1d5a86" stroke-width="1.5"/>
    <text x="320" y="67" font-size="17" font-weight="600" fill="#ffffff">Build</text>
    <rect x="405" y="196" width="150" height="50" rx="9" fill="#eef2f7" stroke="#33506e" stroke-width="1.5"/>
    <text x="480" y="227" font-size="17" fill="#1d2b3a">Check</text>
    <rect x="85" y="196" width="150" height="50" rx="9" fill="#eef2f7" stroke="#33506e" stroke-width="1.5"/>
    <text x="160" y="227" font-size="17" fill="#1d2b3a">Revise</text>
  </g>
</svg>
</div>
```

### A workflow for infectious disease modelling

![A workflow for infectious disease modelling. Figure by Abbott et al. (MIT licence).](figures/workflow-diagram.png){height="460px"}

### Reading the workflow

![](figures/workflow-diagram.png){height="340px"}

1. **Question & estimands** — infections and $R_t$

### Process model

![](figures/workflow-diagram.png){height="340px"}

2. **Process model** — one shared renewal process

### Data source selection

![](figures/workflow-diagram.png){height="340px"}

3. **Data source selection** — catalogue each data source and how it is biased

### Observation model per source

![](figures/workflow-diagram.png){height="340px"}

4. **Observation model per source** — a delay + a scaling + a likelihood

### What is a DAG?

A **directed acyclic graph** draws a model as a picture.
Each quantity is a **node**, and an **arrow** runs from a quantity to whatever it generates.

- Read top to bottom, it traces how the data come about
- The arrows make the model's **conditional independence** assumptions explicit

The DAG *is* the TLDR of the model.

### Modularise

![](figures/workflow-diagram.png){height="340px"}

5. **Modularise** — split into sub-models that can be checked on their own

### Inference & computation choices

![](figures/workflow-diagram.png){height="340px"}

6. **Inference & computation choices** — how to fit the model

### Implementation

![](figures/workflow-diagram.png){height="340px"}

7. **Implementation** — write the model

### Model validation

![](figures/workflow-diagram.png){height="340px"}

8. **Model validation** — check each piece against data

### Data integration

![](figures/workflow-diagram.png){height="340px"}

9. **Data integration** — how to combine the data sources; conflict reveals misspecification

### The question

Our target is unchanged: recover the latent **infections** and **$R_t$** as quickly and with as little bias as we can.

### Now we introduce our data sources

To answer that question we bring in *several* data sources at once, each a delayed, scaled view of the same infections:

- **Cases** — timely, but depend on testing; only a fraction ascertained
- **Deaths** — stable, but long-delayed and severity-dependent
- **Wastewater** — independent of care-seeking; *thought to lead* cases, but indirect, noisy, and on its own scale

## Wastewater: a tangent

### What is wastewater surveillance? {.smaller}

Measuring pathogen RNA shed into sewage — a **population-level** signal.

Why people are interested:

- Does **not** depend on testing, care-seeking, or ascertainment
- **Thought to lead** case reports, and picks up **asymptomatic** infection
- Relatively **cheap** at scale (one sample covers a whole catchment)

. . .

Challenges: it is **noisy** and **indirect**, and needs **scaling / normalisation** before it can be compared to infections.

### A DAG for wastewater

From infections to what we measure:

```{=html}
<div style="display:flex;justify-content:center;margin-top:0.4em;">
<svg viewBox="0 0 300 470" width="100%" style="max-width:250px;font-family:inherit;" role="img" aria-label="A wastewater observation DAG: infections drive shedding of viral load, which is transported and diluted through the sewer system, then sub-sampled and measured in the lab to give the observed concentration.">
  <defs>
    <marker id="ww-arrow" viewBox="0 0 10 10" refX="8.5" refY="5" markerWidth="7" markerHeight="7" orient="auto-start-reverse">
      <path d="M0,0 L10,5 L0,10 z" fill="#5b6b7b"/>
    </marker>
  </defs>
  <g stroke="#5b6b7b" stroke-width="2" fill="none" marker-end="url(#ww-arrow)">
    <path d="M150,58 L150,86"/>
    <path d="M150,142 L150,170"/>
    <path d="M150,226 L150,254"/>
    <path d="M150,310 L150,338"/>
  </g>
  <g text-anchor="middle">
    <rect x="35" y="8" width="230" height="50" rx="9" fill="#2c7fb8" stroke="#1d5a86" stroke-width="1.5"/>
    <text x="150" y="38" font-size="15" font-weight="600" fill="#ffffff">Infections</text>
    <rect x="35" y="92" width="230" height="50" rx="9" fill="#eef2f7" stroke="#33506e" stroke-width="1.5"/>
    <text x="150" y="115" font-size="14" fill="#1d2b3a">Shedding</text>
    <text x="150" y="132" font-size="11" fill="#5b6b7b">(viral load, delayed)</text>
    <rect x="35" y="176" width="230" height="50" rx="9" fill="#eef2f7" stroke="#33506e" stroke-width="1.5"/>
    <text x="150" y="199" font-size="14" fill="#1d2b3a">Sewer transport</text>
    <text x="150" y="216" font-size="11" fill="#5b6b7b">(dilution, decay)</text>
    <rect x="35" y="260" width="230" height="50" rx="9" fill="#eef2f7" stroke="#33506e" stroke-width="1.5"/>
    <text x="150" y="283" font-size="14" fill="#1d2b3a">Sampling &amp; assay</text>
    <text x="150" y="300" font-size="11" fill="#5b6b7b">(sub-sample, measure)</text>
    <rect x="35" y="344" width="230" height="50" rx="9" fill="#ffffff" stroke="#33506e" stroke-width="1.5"/>
    <text x="150" y="367" font-size="14" fill="#1d2b3a">Observed concentration</text>
    <text x="150" y="384" font-size="11" fill="#5b6b7b">~ LogNormal</text>
  </g>
</svg>
</div>
```

## Back to joint modelling

### Three data sources from one trajectory

```{r streams, echo = FALSE, fig.width = 9, fig.height = 4.2}
## reuse the same shared infections the course uses elsewhere
infections <- make_daily_infections(infection_times)$infections
n <- length(infections)

case_delay  <- censored_delay_pmf(rgamma, max = 10, shape = 3,  rate = 1)
death_delay <- censored_delay_pmf(rgamma, max = 21, shape = 16, rate = 1)
ww_delay    <- censored_delay_pmf(rgamma, max = 10, shape = 2,  rate = 1)

## scalings chosen only so the illustrative count streams are visible
obs <- tibble(
  day = seq_len(n),
  infections = infections,
  cases  = rpois(n, 0.4  * convolve_with_delay(infections, case_delay)),
  deaths = rpois(n, 0.05 * convolve_with_delay(infections, death_delay)),
  ww     = rnorm(n, log(2 * convolve_with_delay(infections, ww_delay)), 0.2)
)

obs |>
  pivot_longer(c(infections, cases, deaths, ww),
               names_to = "stream", values_to = "value") |>
  mutate(stream = factor(stream,
    levels = c("infections", "cases", "deaths", "ww"),
    labels = c("Infections (latent)", "Cases", "Deaths", "Wastewater (log)"))) |>
  ggplot(aes(day, value)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 0.8, colour = "#447099") +
  facet_wrap(~stream, scales = "free_y", nrow = 1) +
  labs(x = "Day", y = NULL)
```

Each data source is a delayed, rescaled echo of the same infection curve.

### Steps 2–4 in one picture {.smaller}

We defined the **question** (step 1). This is **steps 2–4**: the shared process, the data sources, and an observation model for each — every data source a convolution of the shared latent infections, conditionally independent given $I$.

$$
\text{cases}_t \sim f\big(\text{convolve}(I, p_\text{cases})\big), \quad
\text{deaths}_t \sim g\big(\text{convolve}(I, p_\text{deaths})\big)
$$

```{=html}
<div style="display:flex;justify-content:center;margin-top:0.4em;">
<svg viewBox="0 0 880 380" width="100%" style="max-width:800px;font-family:inherit;" role="img" aria-label="A shared latent infection process drives cases, deaths and wastewater, each through its own delay and scaling.">
  <defs>
    <marker id="mds-arrow" viewBox="0 0 10 10" refX="8.5" refY="5" markerWidth="7" markerHeight="7" orient="auto-start-reverse">
      <path d="M0,0 L10,5 L0,10 z" fill="#5b6b7b"/>
    </marker>
  </defs>
  <g stroke="#5b6b7b" stroke-width="2" fill="none" marker-end="url(#mds-arrow)">
    <path d="M440,74 L440,142"/>
    <path d="M400,200 C300,238 205,244 162,284"/>
    <path d="M440,202 L440,284"/>
    <path d="M480,200 C580,238 685,244 728,284"/>
  </g>
  <g font-size="13.5" fill="#374553" text-anchor="middle" paint-order="stroke" stroke="#ffffff" stroke-width="4" stroke-linejoin="round">
    <text x="243" y="250">delay + ascertainment</text>
    <text x="470" y="250">delay + IFR</text>
    <text x="648" y="250">delay + scaling</text>
  </g>
  <g text-anchor="middle">
    <rect x="318" y="22" width="244" height="52" rx="9" fill="#eef2f7" stroke="#33506e" stroke-width="1.5"/>
    <text x="440" y="53" font-size="16" fill="#1d2b3a">R<tspan baseline-shift="sub" font-size="11">t</tspan> (geometric random walk)</text>
    <rect x="303" y="146" width="274" height="56" rx="9" fill="#2c7fb8" stroke="#1d5a86" stroke-width="1.5"/>
    <text x="440" y="180" font-size="16.5" font-weight="600" fill="#ffffff">Shared latent infections I</text>
    <rect x="62" y="286" width="200" height="56" rx="9" fill="#eef2f7" stroke="#33506e" stroke-width="1.5"/>
    <text x="162" y="320" font-size="16" fill="#1d2b3a">Cases</text>
    <rect x="340" y="286" width="200" height="56" rx="9" fill="#eef2f7" stroke="#33506e" stroke-width="1.5"/>
    <text x="440" y="320" font-size="16" fill="#1d2b3a">Deaths</text>
    <rect x="628" y="286" width="200" height="56" rx="9" fill="#eef2f7" stroke="#33506e" stroke-width="1.5"/>
    <text x="728" y="320" font-size="16" fill="#1d2b3a">Wastewater</text>
  </g>
</svg>
</div>
```

### Build it in parts

1. **Each data source on its own** — does it recover infections through its delay and scaling?
2. **Link two data sources** — cases + deaths share one infection process
3. **Add the third** — wastewater too; infections and $R_t$ informed by all
4. **Stress-test** — what happens when data sources *conflict*?

### Each data source implies its own answer to "what did infections look like?"

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="42%"}
- changing ascertainment (testing policy)
- mis-specified delays or scalings (e.g. drifting IFR)
- truly different populations
:::

::: {.column width="58%"}

```{r conflict-fork, echo = FALSE, fig.width = 7, fig.height = 6}
## Schematic: each stream implies its own infection trajectory. Up to the peak
## they agree; after it the constant-scaling deaths imply a rising epidemic while
## cases and wastewater imply a falling one -- no single curve can satisfy both.
day <- 1:40
peak <- 20
shared <- 100 * dnorm(day, peak, 7) / max(dnorm(day, peak, 7)) + 5
cases_implied  <- shared
deaths_implied <- ifelse(day <= peak, shared, shared[peak] * (1 + 0.05 * (day - peak)))

fork <- tibble(
  day,
  `implied by cases + wastewater` = cases_implied,
  `implied by deaths` = deaths_implied
) |>
  pivot_longer(-day, names_to = "stream", values_to = "infections")

gap <- tibble(day, lo = pmin(cases_implied, deaths_implied),
              hi = pmax(cases_implied, deaths_implied)) |>
  filter(day >= peak)

ggplot(fork, aes(day, infections, colour = stream)) +
  geom_ribbon(data = gap, inherit.aes = FALSE,
              aes(day, ymin = lo, ymax = hi), fill = "#c0392b", alpha = 0.12) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.2) +
  annotate("text", x = 30, y = 150,
           label = "no single infection curve\ncan satisfy both",
           colour = "#c0392b", size = 4.5, lineheight = 0.95) +
  scale_colour_manual(
    values = c("implied by cases + wastewater" = "#447099",
               "implied by deaths" = "#c0392b")) +
  labs(x = "Day", y = "Implied infections", colour = NULL) +
  theme(legend.position = "top")
```

:::

::::

### Conflict distorts the joint fit

A single shared $I$ can't be both falling (cases, wastewater) and rising (deaths), so the model **splits the difference**.

```{r conflict-distort, echo = FALSE, fig.width = 11, fig.height = 5}
## Schematic: the shared fit is pulled away from the true (declining) trajectory
## towards a compromise with the conflicting (rising) deaths signal.
day <- 1:40
peak <- 20
true <- 100 * dnorm(day, peak, 7) / max(dnorm(day, peak, 7)) + 5
rising <- true
rising[day >= peak] <- true[peak] * (1 + 0.04 * (day[day >= peak] - peak))
compromise <- (true + rising) / 2

distort <- tibble(
  day,
  `true (cases + wastewater)` = true,
  `implied by deaths` = rising,
  `joint fit (compromise)` = compromise
) |>
  pivot_longer(-day, names_to = "trajectory", values_to = "infections") |>
  mutate(trajectory = factor(trajectory, levels = c(
    "true (cases + wastewater)", "implied by deaths", "joint fit (compromise)"
  )))

ggplot(distort, aes(day, infections, colour = trajectory, linetype = trajectory)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.2) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = c(
    "true (cases + wastewater)" = "#447099",
    "implied by deaths" = "#c0392b",
    "joint fit (compromise)" = "#7f4f24"
  )) +
  scale_linetype_manual(values = c("solid", "solid", "dashed")) +
  labs(x = "Day", y = "Implied infections", colour = NULL, linetype = NULL) +
  theme(legend.position = "top")
```

## `r fontawesome::fa("laptop-code", "white")` Your Turn {background-color="#447099" transition="fade-in"}

1. Simulate parallel data sources from one shared infection trajectory
2. Fit each data source alone, then link them through the shared infections
3. Recover infections and $R_t$ from the joint fit
4. Make the deaths conflict, see the joint model surface it, then resolve it by modelling the mechanism

#

[Return to the session](../multiple-data-sources)
